Building a Self-Service Platform

3-year journey from prototype to production | Product Design & Strategy

2022-2025 UX Designer → Sole Product Designer 4 Apps Designed
3 Years of Evolution
4 Major Evolutions
∞ Learnings

The Challenge

Enable users to create photorealistic simulation scenarios for training embodied AI without requiring specialized Unreal Engine expertise or constant engineering support.

My Role

Evolved from UX Designer to Sole Product Designer to Co-Product Ownership, working directly with CPO, PM, and Director of Engineering to shape product strategy.

The Impact

User feedback transformed from bug reports to feature suggestions, signaling a fundamental shift in product maturity and usability.

The Starting Point: Version 1

Hand-Coding Python

Every scenario change required writing pure Python code. Impossible to visualize changes without launching the full 3D application.

Batch File Launches

No friendly interface to launch the simulator. Users had to run batch files with no intuitive entry point.

Specialized Artists Required

All digital twins required Unreal Engine expertise to create or adapt. No self-service option for users who wanted to integrate new assets.

No Organization System

Digital twins stored in large Unreal Engine files with no metadata or organizational system beyond filenames and dates.

The Web Platform Experiment

2021 - The First Pivot

The Hypothesis

Create a web-based 2D platform to simplify scenario creation. Use WebGL to render simulations and lower the barrier to entry for users who weren't familiar with Unreal Engine.

The constraint for the scene to be composed in 2d space was given by the engineering lead after evaluating the skills and time we had to develop the platform.

What We Learned

  • • WebGL couldn't handle our high-fidelity graphics
  • • We'd essentially be building our own game engine
  • • Engineering cost was unsustainable

Don't rebuild what already exists at scale

The following screenshots represent the prototype I shared at an on-site meeting. It shows navigation, edge cases, and methods of composing a 3d scene in 2d space.

Early sketches exploring how to compose 3D scenes in a 2D interface. The notes capture edge cases: prohibited zones around placed objects, collision shapes, and logic for dropping objects onto the nearest surface.

These sketches reveal the inherent complexity of flattening 3D space into 2D views. To help users understand the spatial layout, I explored providing multiple, auto-generated perspective views. But this approach had limitations: it didn't scale for indoor spaces or environments with many obstacles.

Partnering with Unreal Engine

2022 - The Strategic Shift

The Decision

Instead of competing with existing 3d software, embrace it. Partner with Epic to create a custom scenario editor built on their foundation.

My Approach

Digital Twin Terminology

Redesigned UI language to use "digital twin" terminology familiar to our users instead of Unreal Engine jargon

USDA File System Mapping

Mapped our USDA file system directly to the interface, making the technical architecture visible and understandable

Visual Python Generation

Created input systems that generated Python scripts behind the scenes - users configure visually, code writes itself

Simplifying the Outliner Structure

Unreal Engine

Complex structure used for game development

Scenario Builder

Simplified structure based on digital twin simulation terminology

Creating a Focused Asset Browser

Unreal Engine

Content browser shows all project files, making it difficult to find simulation-specific assets

Scenario Builder

Falcon Browser only contains digital twins, making scenario building more straightforward and focused on simulation

Fast Mockups

Recreating the UE interface in Figma allowed for rapid prototyping, iteration, and quicker specification development

Hybrid Platform Strategy

2023 - The Layered Approach

The Realization

Unreal Engine has a steep learning curve. Our platform is inherently complex. We needed layered support to serve different user needs.

The Solution

Stop fighting the complexity - embrace it with complementary platforms.

Web App (Supporting)

  • • Documentation where it's easy to update
  • • Onboarding flows for new users
  • • Lightweight access for stakeholder demos

Native App (Power)

  • • Full scenario editing capabilities
  • • High-fidelity simulation rendering
  • • Offline capability for secure projects

The Offline Reality

2024 - Serving All Users

The Challenge

Many clients operate in air-gapped, internet-free environments. We couldn't depend solely on the web platform.
How do you serve vastly different user needs in one interface without bloat?

The Solution: A Launcher for Two Worlds

Users who are only interested in making a scenario for simulation can immediately get started with the primary green button. They can also easily access guides and tutorials that exist on our webapp.

We preserve all existing Unreal Engine functionality and users can access the original launcher through the "Unreal Engine Projects" button.

Concept Mock

What I Learned

1. Self-Service is Strategic, Not Cosmetic

Reducing service dependency fundamentally changes the business model. We couldn't eliminate high-touch clients (our highest revenue), but we also couldn't neglect lower-tier users.

The challenge: How do you sufficiently support all user segments without diluting the product?

2. Mental Models Scale Culture

As the product matured, inconsistent terminology and system understanding across the team created friction.

The solution: I established clear communication of mental models, distinguishing between research/discovery work (subject to change) and committed system design (requires consistency). This built robustness and reduced rework.

3. Complexity Can't Be Wished Away

Inherently complex workflows resist over-simplification. "Make it one button" often creates abstraction that leads to confusion.

The insight: The real design challenge is deciding what to simplify and what to expose. Every compromise has tradeoffs.

Outcomes & Impact

✨

User feedback transformed from bug reports to feature suggestions

This shift indicates users moved from "can't use it" to "want more from it"
A fundamental product maturity milestone

Before

Prior to scenario editor

"It doesn't feel like a complete product"

"Hacky, not out-of-the-box"

"Some things just didn't work and I don't know why"

"Things I learned didn't matter after the next release"

"None of what I had to learn felt transferable"

After

First scenario editor release

"Seems strictly better in most cases to use the UI over editing USDA"

"I feel fairly confident in my ability to move an asset to twin and utilize that asset"

Maturity

Users now asking for more

"Where can I read about how to get the most out of your API?"

"Can I use my own file versioning system?"

"Can you add IR sensors?"

Self-Service Enabled

Users can create scenarios more independently

Support Dependency Reduced

Lower-tier users no longer require constant engineering help

Multi-Platform Ecosystem

Seamless experience across web, native, and offline environments

Designing a Scenario and Digital Twin Management Webapp

Managed product development and designed a complete web-based experience from scratch. A Vue.js web application.

Learn More

Developing and Maintaining the Duality Marketing Website

Handled UX design, Webflow development, and team training for the Falcon website. Collaborated on site flow and structure, implemented designs in Webflow, and educated colleagues on CMS usage.
